source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC

extracts.extract: Investigating the digital means taking nothing at face value. It means understanding that things which appear to be quantifiable and data-driven — likes, shares, retweets, traffic, product reviews, advertising clicks — are easily and often manipulated.
extracts.notes: Core epistemological stance: all quantitative digital signals can be gamed
extracts.relevance_rationale: Digital signals being manipulable is even more true now that LLMs can generate fake engagement at scale; needs AI-specific examples

extracts.extract: Think like an adversary. Each new feature of a platform or digital service can be exploited in some way. It's critical to put yourself in the shoes of someone looking to manipulate the environment for ideological, political, financial or other reasons. When you look at digital content and messages, you should consider the motivations driving its creation and propagation.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Adversarial thinking now must account for LLM-powered manipulation campaigns that exploit platforms at unprecedented scale and sophistication

extracts.extract: Focus on actors, content, behavior and networks. The goal is to analyze the actors, content and behavior and how they are to document how they might be working in unison as a network.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Analyzing actors, content, behavior and networks endures but must now include AI-generated content and LLM-operated bot networks

extracts.extract: Monitor and collect. The best way to identify media manipulation and disinformation is to look for it all the time. Ongoing monitoring and tracking of known actors, topics and communities of interest is essential. Keep and organize what you find, whether in spreadsheets, screenshot folders or by using paid tools like Hunchly.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ongoing monitoring endures but must now incorporate AI-generated content detection and LLM-powered monitoring tools

extracts.extract: Be careful with attribution. It's sometimes impossible to say exactly who's behind a particular account, piece of content, or a larger information operation. One reason is that actors with different motives can behave in similar ways, and produce or amplify the same kind of content. Even the platforms themselves — which have far better access to data and more resources — make attribution mistakes. The most successful and compelling evidence usually combines digital proof with information from inside sources — an ideal mix of online and traditional investigative work.
extracts.notes: Digital + traditional methods needed for reliable attribution
extracts.relevance_rationale: Caution about attribution remains critical; LLMs make it even easier for different actors to mimic each others signals

extracts.extract: At First Draft, we talk about the concept of the tipping point. For journalists, reporting on falsehoods too early provides additional and potentially damaging oxygen to a rumor. Reporting too late means it has taken hold and there is little that can be done. Working out that tipping point is challenging. It differs by location, topic and platform.
extracts.notes: No universal rule for when to debunk
extracts.relevance_rationale: The tipping point concept for when to report on falsehoods is a timeless editorial judgment framework

extracts.extract: The life cycle has five points of action, where the tactics of media manipulators can be documented using qualitative and quantitative methods. Note that most manipulation campaigns are not 'discovered' in this order. Instead when researching, look for any one of these points of action and then trace the campaign backward and forward through the life cycle.
extracts.notes: Five stages: planning, seeding, responses, ecosystem changes, adjustments
extracts.relevance_rationale: The lifecycle framework for media manipulation endures but must incorporate AI-generated content as a new vector at each lifecycle stage

extracts.extract: Grappling with the content and the context of disinformation requires us all to forensically document with rigor how campaigns start, change and end. And to recognize that every perceived ending of a campaign may very well be a new beginning.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Forensically documenting campaign lifecycles with rigor and recognizing campaigns never truly end are timeless analytical principles

extracts.extract: First, I plug the username into Google. People — especially younger ones who eschew the larger social platforms — tend to leave a trail even in more unexpected places, including comment sections, reviews and forums, that can lead you to information and other accounts.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Username tracing across platforms endures but LLM-powered OSINT tools now automate cross-platform identity resolution

extracts.extract: When I find a username or email I think might belong to my subject, I plug it into an online tool like namechk or namecheckr that looks for username availability across multiple platforms. These tools are designed to be an easy way for marketers to see if a given username they're planning to register is available across platforms. But they're also useful for checking whether a username you're investigating also exists elsewhere.
extracts.notes: Username match across platforms does not prove same person
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tools like namechk/namecheckr are displaced by LLM agents that can check username availability across platforms programmatically

extracts.extract: Google's reverse image search is fine, but often other search engines — especially Russia's Yandex — may deliver better results. I use the Reveye Chrome extension, which allows me to right click on an image and search for its match across multiple platforms including Google, Bing, Yandex and Tineye.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reverse image search across engines endures but must now include AI-powered visual search tools beyond Google and Yandex

extracts.extract: I don't just trust my eyes. Identifying characteristics across photos like moles or facial hair or features is helpful; lately, I also like to check it with a facial recognition tool like Face++, which allows you to upload two photos and then gives a probability that those belong to the same person.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using facial recognition tools like Face++ needs updating for newer AI models and ethical concerns about facial recognition technology

extracts.extract: It's not just profile photos that can be signposts, however. As people become more aware of and concerned with their own privacy and that of their family, they're still inclined to share photos of things they're proud of. I've identified people by connecting photos of things like cars, homes or pets. In this sense, photos become a means to connect accounts and the people behind them to one another, enabling you to build out the network around your target.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using pets, possessions, and location clues to link accounts exploits human behavioral patterns that persist regardless of technology

extracts.extract: With a new investigation, I like to start at the beginning of someone's Twitter history and work forward in time. You can get there by hand, with an assist from an autoscroller chrome extension, or you can use Twitter's advanced search to limit the time frame to the first few months of an account's existence.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Chronological account review endures but LLMs can now analyze entire account histories instantly rather than manual scrolling

extracts.extract: Because of that truth, it's always illuminating to look at an account's earliest followers and friends. TweetBeaver is a good tool for investigating the connections between large accounts and for downloading things like timelines and favorites of smaller accounts. For larger datasets, I rely on a developer with API access.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Analyzing earliest followers and connections is still valuable but specific tools like TweetBeaver may be defunct; LLMs enable network analysis at scale

extracts.extract: To find recently deleted tweets, you can search Google's cache; older deleted tweets can also sometimes be accessed in the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine or another archive. The manual archive site archive.is turns up several deleted tweets.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Searching caches and archives for deleted content is a timeless investigative technique; platforms change but the principle persists

extracts.extract: Confirming a social account with the subject, family and friends, law enforcement and/or social media PR are ways to protect yourself from being duped.
extracts.notes: Applies especially in breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: Confirming accounts with subjects, family, and law enforcement is traditional verification that no tool replaces

extracts.extract: Whether it's tracking your steps in a Google doc or letting a paid tool like Hunchly monitor as you search — is the key to clarifying connections between people and the lives they lead online, and turning those conclusions into stories.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documentation of investigative steps endures but tools like Hunchly may be dated; LLM-assisted research logging is now available

extracts.extract: Our first step in investigating the set of accounts was to try to verify if they were connected to real people. This part required good old fashioned fact-checking and began with our creating spreadsheets to track details related to the accounts, including the personal details they listed, the pages they liked and other information.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Combining old-fashioned fact-checking with spreadsheet-based systematic tracking of accounts is enduring methodology

extracts.extract: Using reverse image search tools, we found that many of 26 accounts used profile photos of celebrities or personalities.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reverse image search to identify stolen profile photos endures but now must account for AI-generated profile photos that evade reverse search

extracts.extract: Fake accounts are often used to spam groups with links, and you can sometimes catch them reusing the same text when they do it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Detecting link spam patterns and reused text across fake accounts remains a valid investigative technique

extracts.extract: We also found that OKD2.com shared the same Google AdSense ID as other websites, such as askphilippines.com and why0why.com, that the 26 accounts were sharing. We identified the AdSense IDs on these sites by viewing the source code of pages on them and looking for a series of numbers that began with the letters 'ca-pub-.' Each Google AdSense account is given a unique ID that begins with 'ca-pub-,' and each page of a site that is linked to an account will have this code on it.
extracts.notes: Key technique for connecting seemingly separate websites
extracts.relevance_rationale: Tracing shared ad/analytics IDs across websites to expose network connections is a durable forensic technique

extracts.extract: Developing sources at tech companies who are willing to tell you more information than the company will say on the record is becoming increasingly important as many stories cannot be uncovered purely using open source information as scammers and bad actors become more sophisticated.
extracts.chapter: Case Study: Fake Black Lives Matter page
extracts.relevance_rationale: Developing human sources at tech companies is increasingly important as platforms become more opaque; no AI substitute exists

extracts.extract: What was particularly notable about this story was the array of techniques that Massler and I used to get it over the line. We relied heavily on archive sites like the Wayback Machine that allowed us to see the look of websites the page had been linking to and the page itself before it came on our radar. This was particularly useful, as after Massler initially contacted MacKay the people behind the page began trying to cover some of their tracks.
extracts.chapter: Case Study: Fake Black Lives Matter page
extracts.notes: Subjects may destroy evidence once aware of investigation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Combining archive sites, source code analysis, and traditional reporting is an enduring multi-method investigative approach

extracts.extract: The simple act of picking up the phone to talk to MacKay and developing sources to provide information that would otherwise not be made public — traditional journalism techniques — were critical in exposing this scam.
extracts.chapter: Case Study: Fake Black Lives Matter page
extracts.notes: OSINT alone insufficient; traditional techniques remain essential
extracts.relevance_rationale: Traditional journalism techniques like picking up the phone remain critical for exposing what digital-only methods cannot

extracts.extract: By default, you should distrust search engines from primary sources. They can give you a false impression of the actual content of the website and its associated databases. The proper way to search is to perform a 'primary source check.'
extracts.notes: Site-internal search often returns incomplete results
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distrusting search engine results from primary sources and checking actual database content is a timeless verification principle

extracts.extract: Go to a generic search engine. Start with the query and end with 'site:' followed directly with the URL (no spaces). This is the formula for finding out if an original source shows you everything.
extracts.notes: Primary source check formula: query + site:domain
extracts.relevance_rationale: Site-specific search operator syntax is displaced by LLM search tools that handle domain-scoped queries conversationally

extracts.extract: Try to find the common denominator of any online invitation. It's often a PDF document. Search for just that with 'filetype:pdf' and you might find it.
extracts.notes: Part of a 3-step document trail method: establish type, be neutral on wording, identify who is involved
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filetype-specific search operators are displaced by LLM agents that find and parse documents across formats conversationally

extracts.extract: Generic search engines like Google often struggle to deliver quality results from the trillions of posts on Twitter, or on big platforms such as Facebook and Instagram. The answer for Twitter is to use its Advanced Search functionality and add keywords, username and time period.
extracts.notes: Google site: operator unreliable for social media platforms
extracts.relevance_rationale: Platform-specific search tools like TweetDeck advanced search are displaced by LLM-powered social media analysis tools

extracts.extract: Exclude the target website by adding -site:friendsofscience.org. This helps you find information from outside parties.
extracts.notes: Excluding target site reveals third-party references
extracts.relevance_rationale: Site exclusion operators are basic search syntax now handled conversationally by LLM search agents

extracts.extract: In general, we suggest the following approach for identifying inauthentic and potentially automated behavior on social networks: Manually check the accounts for suspicious behavior. Combine this with the use of tools or more technical network analyses. Investigate their activity, content and network of other accounts they interact with. Combine this with traditional investigation techniques, such as trying to contact them or people they claim to know. Consult with outside experts who specialize in bots and inauthentic activity.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.notes: Four-step approach combining manual, technical, traditional, and expert methods
extracts.relevance_rationale: Combined manual and automated bot detection endures but must now account for LLM-powered bots that evade traditional detection heuristics

extracts.extract: Bot detection tools are a great starting point but they should not be your sole evidence. One reason for their varying degree of accuracy is there is simply no universal list of criteria for recognizing bots with 100% certainty.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Bot detection tool limitations are even more pronounced now that LLM-operated accounts blur the line between human and automated behavior

extracts.extract: Journalists must avoid falsely labeling suspicious accounts as bots without proper evidence and analysis, as a mistaken accusation can undermine your credibility.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Avoiding false bot accusations that undermine credibility is a timeless ethical principle of responsible reporting

extracts.extract: One way to deal with these different types of bots, cyborgs and hyperactive human accounts is to focus your investigation on monitoring all inauthentic or bot-like behavior, instead of trying to identify only one type of suspicious account.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Focusing on inauthentic behavior rather than bot identity endures but LLM-generated content blurs the behavior/identity distinction further

extracts.extract: If you come across such an account, analyze the account's tweet activity more deeply. Take the number of tweets located at the top of the page, and divide this by the number of days the account has been active.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.notes: Calculate tweets-per-day to gauge plausibility
extracts.relevance_rationale: Tweet-per-day activity analysis is still useful but LLM bots can now mimic realistic posting frequencies, requiring more sophisticated analysis

extracts.extract: To get a better understanding of the activity of a whole bot network, you can use a visualization platform like Gephi.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Gephi network visualization endures but LLM-powered graph analysis tools can now automate community detection and visualization

extracts.extract: For now, a methodical analysis of account behavior, content, interactions and patterns remains the best approach for identifying inauthentic behavior.
extracts.chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Methodical behavior analysis remains best practice but must now account for LLM-generated content that defeats traditional behavioral signals

extracts.extract: We wanted to focus on pro-China accounts and content, since these were the ones Twitter had already found engaging in inauthentic activity. We tried keyword formulations like: 'Shame on Hong Kong' -police -government. This search yields results that contain the phrase but not the words police or government.
extracts.chapter: Case study: Hong Kong protests Twitter activity
extracts.notes: Negative keywords to isolate pro-government content
extracts.relevance_rationale: Crafting targeted keyword formulations to isolate coordinated activity is enduring investigative craft

extracts.extract: You can filter by engagement simply by adding 'min_retweets:500' or 'min_faves:500' to your search query. This will get only tweets with at least 500 retweets or likes.
extracts.chapter: Case study: Hong Kong protests Twitter activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Platform-specific engagement filter syntax like min_retweets is displaced by LLM tools that query social data conversationally

extracts.extract: The case study shows how it is possible to combine manual and automated techniques to quickly uncover a network of suspicious Twitter accounts. It also illustrates that it is useful to look for additional accounts and activity even after a platform announces a takedown of accounts.
extracts.chapter: Case study: Hong Kong protests Twitter activity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Combining manual and automated techniques and seeking additional evidence beyond platform-provided data is enduring methodology

extracts.extract: The key to monitoring and debunking during breaking news is to lay a strong foundation before it happens. This means having a solid grounding in basic verification skills, understanding how to monitor social networks and platforms, and knowing how to respond if you or your colleagues become targeted by bad actors.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: Laying groundwork for verification before breaking news happens is enduring preparedness principle

extracts.extract: Before looking for hoaxes, set up a folder for your documents and start a spreadsheet for what you find. Immediately take a screenshot of each hoax and relevant piece of content you discover, and archive the page. Be sure to record the original and archived URLs of the content in your spreadsheet.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: Archiving evidence immediately via screenshots and spreadsheets is enduring verification workflow

extracts.extract: To avoid helping spread pages associated with dis- or misinformation, be sure to link to the archived URL in any articles or social media posts instead of the original. It is also a best practice to watermark your images with a clear label such as 'False' or 'Misleading' to ensure they are spread and indexed with the proper context. If you do write an article, focus your headline and copy on what is true, rather than primarily saying what is false. Studies have shown that repeating falsehoods can cause people to retain the incorrect information.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.notes: Truth-first framing reduces retention of falsehoods
extracts.relevance_rationale: Linking to archived URLs to avoid amplifying disinfo and watermarking screenshots are enduring practices

extracts.extract: As the event unfolds, come up with a list of locations and relevant keywords. For location, take into account the city, state and country, and any relevant local terms such as the nickname for a city or affected neighborhood. Note that during stressful situations, people may misspell locations or names. Include common misspellings in your searches and try to identify possible autocorrect mistakes.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: Keyword and location search techniques endure but LLMs can now generate and expand search terms dynamically

extracts.extract: InVID is a free browser extension and the best platform for helping you analyze and verify videos. It allows for users to paste a URL into its engine, which will then extract thumbnails from the video.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: InVID still useful but AI-powered video verification tools are rapidly expanding capabilities

extracts.extract: The best way to search Twitter is by using TweetDeck, which allows you to create unique columns for searches and lists. Finding and duplicating relevant lists is key for staying abreast of a situation. You can use Google to search for Twitter lists using a simple formula: site:twitter.com/*/lists 'Alabama reporters'.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: TweetDeck deprecated; platform monitoring now requires updated tools and approaches for X/Twitter changes

extracts.extract: Has it spread outside of its initial network of sharers? Has it been shared by figures of authority? Has it generated significant engagement? There are no simple rules on when to debunk a falsehood, but some good questions to ask.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.notes: Three questions to assess whether to debunk
extracts.relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment criteria for when to debunk are enduring newsroom decisions unaffected by AI

extracts.extract: After the dust settles, it is helpful to look back at your saved images and posts. While in the moment you want to highlight individual falsehoods by way of public service journalism, in the aftermath you should take stock of any themes or patterns that can be seen. A wrap-up story can help your readers fully grasp the purpose and methods of the disinformation's spread. It will also serve as a research tool for you and your newsroom.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
extracts.relevance_rationale: Post-event pattern analysis and wrap-up reporting is enduring journalistic practice

extracts.extract: To go beyond standard forms of image verification, we have combined methods from art history with questions designed specifically for mis- and disinformation content. Our framework, '20 Questions for Interrogating Social Media Images,' designed collaboratively with First Draft and journalists, is an additional tool journalists can use when investigating images.
extracts.notes: Five core questions: What is it? What does it show? Who made it? What did it mean? What does it mean?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Systematic image questioning frameworks combining art history and verification methods endure as analytical discipline

extracts.extract: Misinformation does not necessarily involve fake images or even the misidentification of what they show. Sometimes images can be used to support a message that misrepresents a wider situation. Some misinformation requires more than verification. There is a need to critically examine how real images are used to manipulate, and what such images do and mean.
extracts.notes: Verification alone is insufficient; meaning analysis is needed
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding that misinfo can use real images in misleading context is enduring media literacy insight

extracts.extract: Journalists should think carefully about bringing further attention to such emotionally fueled, controversial and potentially harmful disinformation by reporting on it, even with positive intentions.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment about amplification risk and harm reduction in reporting on disinfo is enduring

extracts.extract: Technical approaches are useful until synthetic media techniques inevitably adapt to them. A perfect deepfake detection system will never exist.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Deepfake detection cat-and-mouse intensified by LLMs; no perfect detection is even more true now

extracts.extract: Journalists and investigators need to establish a mentality of measured skepticism around photos, videos and audio. They must assume that these forms of media will be challenged more frequently as knowledge and fear of deepfakes increases. It is also essential to develop a strong familiarity with media forensics tools.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Measured skepticism toward media now must account for far more sophisticated LLM-generated synthetic content

extracts.extract: An approach to analyzing and verifying deepfakes and synthetic media manipulation should include: 1. Reviewing the content for synthetic media-derived telltale glitches or distortions. 2. Applying existing video verification and forensics approaches. 3. Utilizing emerging new AI-based approaches and emerging forensics approaches when available.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Three-step deepfake approach needs updating as LLM-era synthetic media may lack traditional glitches

extracts.extract: Since most deepfakes or modifications are currently not fully synthesized but instead rely on making changes in a source video, you can use frames from a video to look for other versions using a reverse image search. You can also check the video to see if the landscape and landmarks are consistent with images of the same location in Google Street View.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reverse image search for deepfakes still useful but fully synthesized content now more common than modified source video

extracts.extract: Given the nature of both media forensics and emerging deepfakes technologies, we have to accept that the absence of evidence that something was tampered with will not be conclusive proof that media has not been tampered with.
extracts.notes: Absence of tampering evidence is not proof of authenticity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Absence of tampering evidence not proving authenticity is an enduring epistemological truth amplified by AI

extracts.extract: The best way to approach verification is using existing approaches to image/video, and complement these with forensics tools that can detect image manipulation. Trusting the human eye is not a robust strategy!
extracts.relevance_rationale: Not trusting the human eye is even more critical now; AI detection tools are essential but also rapidly evolving

extracts.extract: Before trying this, you should talk to your editor or manager about how to answer these questions. Will you be truthful about who you are and why you are in the group? Is there a way to join by staying deliberately vague? If not, how can you justify that decision to hide your identity?
extracts.chapter: Monitoring and Reporting Inside Closed Groups and Messaging Apps
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ethical deliberation about identity disclosure when entering closed groups is enduring editorial practice

extracts.extract: The other technique is to set up a tipline, where you encourage the public to send you content. The key to a tipline is having a simple, clear call to action, and that you explain how you intend to use that content.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring and Reporting Inside Closed Groups and Messaging Apps
extracts.notes: Tiplines can be gamed; transparency increases participation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Setting up tiplines with clear calls to action and transparency about data use is enduring engagement practice

extracts.extract: Reporting from and about closed messaging apps and groups is full of challenges, yet those sources will become increasingly important as spaces where information is shared. As a first step, think about the questions outlined in this chapter, talk to your colleagues and editors, and if you don't have guidelines in your newsroom about this type of reporting, start working on some.
extracts.chapter: Monitoring and Reporting Inside Closed Groups and Messaging Apps
extracts.relevance_rationale: Developing newsroom guidelines for reporting from closed messaging apps is enduring editorial need

extracts.extract: Unable to identify the authors of the recording, Comprova focused on a more conventional investigation, and requested the help of an expert report from Instituto Brasileiro de Pericia (the Brazilian Institute of Forensics). Experts compared the viral recording with Bolsonaro's voice in an April 2018 interview and concluded that the voice of the candidate was not the voice on the recording.
extracts.chapter: Case Study: Bolsonaro at the Hospital
extracts.notes: When digital provenance fails, use forensic analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Audio forensics via expert analysis endures but AI voice analysis tools now supplement traditional expert reports

extracts.extract: Remember that text, images or the entire site itself may disappear over time -- especially after you start contacting people and asking questions. A best practice is to use the Wayback Machine to save important pages on your target website as part of your workflow.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Archiving websites via Wayback Machine before contacting subjects is enduring investigative practice

extracts.extract: This chapter will look at four categories to analyze when investigating a website: content, code, analytics, registration and connected elements.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Four-category framework for website investigation is enduring analytical methodology

extracts.extract: An important part of examining the content is to see if it is original. Has text from the site's About page or other general text been copied from elsewhere? Is the site spreading false or misleading information, or helping push a specific agenda?
extracts.notes: Copy-paste text into Google to check originality
extracts.relevance_rationale: Plagiarism detection on websites needs updating as LLMs can generate original-sounding text that evades traditional checks

extracts.extract: This approach, first discovered by Lawrence Alexander, begins with viewing the source code of a webpage and then searching within it to see if you can locate a Google Analytics and/or Google AdSense code. These are hugely popular products from Google that enable a site owner to track stats or earn money from ads. If someone is running multiple sites, they often use the same Analytics or AdSense account to manage them. This provides an investigator with the opportunity to connect seemingly separate sites by finding the same ID in the source code.
extracts.notes: All Analytics IDs begin with ua-; AdSense IDs have pub-
extracts.relevance_rationale: Google Analytics/AdSense ID tracing endures in principle but platform changes may alter technical specifics

extracts.extract: One caveat is that it is relatively inexpensive to pay to have your personal information privacy protected when you register a domain. Even in those cases, a whois search will still tell us the date the domain was most recently registered, when it will expire and the IP address on the internet where the site is hosted.
extracts.relevance_rationale: WHOIS lookups and domain registration research remain relevant investigative techniques

extracts.extract: As for IP addresses, beware that completely unconnected websites can be hosted on the same server. A general rule is that the fewer the websites hosted on the same server, the more likely they may be connected.
extracts.notes: Shared IP is weak signal; fewer co-hosted sites = stronger signal
extracts.relevance_rationale: Caution about shared hosting false positives in IP analysis is enduring technical awareness

extracts.extract: Ad libraries are nevertheless an important step toward more transparency and provide journalists and others with exciting new ways of investigating digital advertisements.
extracts.notes: Ad libraries available for Google, Facebook, Snapchat, LinkedIn
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ad libraries as transparency tools for investigating digital ads remain important regardless of AI

extracts.extract: Don't trust anything on 4chan/8chan. These websites are literally built to troll, spread innuendo and falsehoods about perceived enemies, push lies about marginalized people. Don't go into a thread asking for more details. Don't post anything, actually. You will be targeted by people with too much time on their hands.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distrust of anonymous platform content and safety awareness on 4chan/8chan is enduring source literacy

extracts.extract: Searching for '[city name] + [8chan or 4chan or incels.co]' or other extremist sites provides a useful template for any similar event.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Search template for extremist platforms during breaking news is enduring investigative technique

extracts.extract: Right-clicking the timestamp and clicking 'inspect element' will bring up the site's source code, and it will highlight a section that starts with '<time unixtime=[number]>.' Copy and paste that number into an Epoch/Unix timestamp converter, and you'll get a to-the-second post timestamp in UTC time.
extracts.notes: Both 4chan and 8chan localize timestamps; inspect element reveals UTC
extracts.relevance_rationale: Unix timestamp inspection technique is platform-specific; concept endures but tools and platforms change

extracts.extract: It is again important to emphasize that the presence of a username across platforms does not guarantee the accounts were created by one person.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Caution that matching usernames across platforms does not prove same person is enduring attribution wisdom

extracts.extract: Changing Google's search parameters to filter by posts in the 'last hour,' then searching 'site:4chan.org polls' will give you a pretty good window into the polls 4chan users are trying to manipulate in real time.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Real-time Google search filtering for 4chan poll manipulation is tool-specific and needs platform updates

extracts.extract: For an investigator, the goal is to find as much of the operation as possible before reporting it, because once the operation is reported, the operators can be expected to hide -- potentially by deleting or abandoning other assets.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Preserving evidence before reporting to prevent operator deletion is enduring investigative tradecraft

extracts.extract: There is no single rule for identifying the first link in the chain by your own resources. The most effective strategy is to look for the incongruous. Taking the time to say 'That is weird: Why is it like that?' can be the first step in exposing a new operation.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Looking for the incongruous as investigation starting point is enduring curiosity-driven methodology

extracts.extract: Three questions are crucial here, modeled on Camille Francois' Disinformation ABC: What information about the initial asset is available? How did the asset behave? What content did the asset produce?
extracts.notes: Assets-Behavior-Content framework for information operations
extracts.relevance_rationale: ABC framework (Assets, Behavior, Content) for network analysis is enduring investigative model

extracts.extract: If an asset shares a text, it is worth searching where else that text appeared. Especially with longer texts, it is advisable to select a sentence or two from the third or fourth paragraphs, or lower, as deceptive operations have been known to edit the headlines and ledes of articles they have copied, but are less likely to take the time to edit the body of the text.
extracts.notes: Search from body text, not headlines, for better matches
extracts.relevance_rationale: Text matching for network mapping endures in principle but LLMs can now paraphrase to avoid detection

extracts.extract: Assets that post text with mistakes have particular value, as errors are, by their nature, more unusual than correctly spelled words. This made for a much more targeted Google search with far fewer results.
extracts.notes: Misspellings narrow search results significantly
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spelling error as unique search identifier may become less reliable as LLMs auto-correct disinfo text

extracts.extract: A key rule of analysis is to record them when you find them, because you may not get a second chance. Whatever the format, some pieces of information should always be recorded. These include how the asset was found, its name and URL, the date it was created, and the number of followers, follows, likes and/or views. They also include a basic description of the asset, to remind you what it was after looking at 500 other assets.
extracts.notes: Record how each asset was found for reproducibility
extracts.relevance_rationale: Recording evidence immediately with full metadata is enduring investigative workflow principle

extracts.extract: It is worth giving the asset a confidence score. The level of confidence (high, moderate or low) should be marked as a separate entry, and the reasons should be added to the notes.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Confidence scoring for assets with explicit reasoning is enduring analytical methodology

extracts.extract: Ultimately, attribution is a question of self-restraint. The researcher has to imagine the question, 'How can you prove that this operation was run by the person you are accusing?' If they cannot answer that question with confidence to themselves, they should steer clear of making the accusation.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Attribution as self-restraint requiring provable evidence is enduring investigative ethics

extracts.extract: Contacting the relevant platforms is a vital part of investigative work. As the platforms have scaled up their internal investigative teams, they have become more willing to offer public attribution for information operations.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Contacting platforms for cooperation on attribution is enduring investigative practice

extracts.extract: Endless Mayfly illustrates how you can combine network and narrative analysis with external reporting to arrive at attribution. It also highlights the difficulty involved in attributing information operations to a specific actor, why multiple indicators are required, and how to use a confidence level to indicate your level of certitude for the attribution.
extracts.notes: Three analysis types: narrative, network, external reporting
extracts.relevance_rationale: Combining network and narrative analysis for attribution with confidence levels is enduring methodology

extracts.extract: Using content and discourse analysis on the 135 inauthentic articles collected in our investigation, we determined that the narratives being propagated were aligned with Iran's interests. Each article was coded into categories that were determined after an initial reading of all the articles. Two rounds of coding were conducted: The first round was executed independently by two researchers, and a second round was conducted together by the same researchers to resolve any discrepancies.
extracts.notes: Standard qualitative coding methodology applied to disinfo
extracts.relevance_rationale: Content/discourse analysis with intercoder reliability for narrative coding is enduring research methodology

extracts.extract: To avoid hasty and poor attribution practices, it is important to consider multiple indicators, types of evidence and analyses, and to make use of a confidence level that considers alternative hypotheses and missing data.
extracts.notes: Uses intelligence-community framework: low/moderate/high confidence
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using multiple indicators, confidence levels, and alternative hypotheses to avoid hasty attribution is enduring standard
